entries.title: A CI gate that fails your PR if it's 15% slower
entries.summary: I fixed a real N+1-shaped bug in my fitness agent's MCP tools, nine database connections opened for one page of coaching advice, and then had to prove it instead of eyeballing it. Here's the CI-gated benchmark that does the proving, and the two ways it almost lied to me.

entries.title: A coaching line that still ships when the model call doesn't
entries.summary: The daily brief PDF grew a Training Plan section with a Claude-written coaching line, and the interesting part isn't the prompt, it's the deterministic fallback that runs when the model call fails.

entries.title: Why a daily sync kept tripping its own rate limit
entries.summary: A scheduled job kept logging a 429, and the cause was the logins, not the data calls. Here is how to make a client resume a saved session instead of re-authenticating on every run, plus the failure modes that quietly send it back to a full login.

entries.title: Let an agent own the output, but verify it three ways before you cut over
entries.summary: This release let the agent produce the daily brief as structured data and cut over to it. Trusting a model with structured output needs more than a schema. Here are the three checks that made the switch safe: a typed schema, an advisory grounding signal, and a shadow run against a baseline.

entries.title: Two free workflows to run before you make a repo public
entries.summary: Before flipping a personal repo to public, turn on the static analysis and supply-chain checks GitHub gives you for free. This release added CodeQL and dependency review. Here is what each one does and how to wire them in.

entries.title: Keeping a long-lived dev branch from drifting after squash merges
entries.summary: Squash-merging a long-lived dev branch into main makes the next promotion show phantom diffs. This release scripted the fix: force-reset dev onto main after each promotion, including the protected-branch dance. Here is the whole thing.

entries.title: Deleting code on purpose, made safe by the tests you write first
entries.summary: This release added a chart tool and removed a pile of unused code at the same time. The interesting part is the order: write the tests that pin what you keep, then delete what you do not need. Here is how to do that without holding your breath.

entries.title: Giving a solo project a real branch model (and why it isn't overkill)
entries.summary: Why a one-person, agent-built project still earns a feature→dev→main branch model with automatic release tagging, the CI wiring that cuts the tag only when the version is new, and the dependency drift a single major bump surfaced.

entries.title: Handing an AI client a persona over MCP, and learning to resolve it late
entries.summary: The coach voice now follows me into Claude Code through the MCP server's instructions field. Getting it right meant resolving the persona at connect time instead of import time, and failing safe when the lookup misses. Here is the full build.

entries.title: Selectable coach tones, and making the model's dials actually do something
entries.summary: The daily brief's voice is now a profile you choose. The real work wasn't the personas, it was making the tuning numbers deterministic instead of decorative, and testing all four tones against expectations rather than reading a sample.

entries.title: A settings resolver that defaults to the present and fails safe on bad input
entries.summary: Five coaching decisions were hardcoded as my preferences. Turning them into user settings came down to one resolution order over three existing config homes, defaults that exactly reproduce the old behavior, and validation that falls back instead of silently grading you wrong.

entries.title: Grade the state, not the calendar: a date is a stand-in for the question you actually care about
entries.summary: Two grading bugs traced to the same mistake: the grader read the calendar instead of the outcome, so today's finished run showed pending and a recovery walk read as a miss. The fix is a small decision table that grades by what actually happened, plus one verdict the UI reads instead of recomputing.

entries.title: Give the agent a tool, not a shell
entries.summary: My fitness agent answered a plan question by opening a shell and writing raw SQL. The fix was not a better prompt, it was a missing tool. Here is the full build of the high-level tool that replaced the improvisation: a data model, a per-day grading rule with one honest boundary, the single tool that returns the whole graded plan, and the test that defends it.

entries.title: The brief got ~2.5-3x faster, and the fancy idea lost to a measurement
entries.summary: The daily brief dropped from about four minutes to roughly 80 seconds at equal-or-better quality, about 2.5-3x. The parallel-fan-out plan I designed lost to a profiler, the real lever was one setting, and less model reasoning made the output better, not worse. Here is the full build: profile the phases, dial reasoning effort to low, render tables in code, and confirm with a blind A/B.

entries.title: When the agent can read and write everything, the server stops running the model
entries.summary: Once the agent can both read and write local-fitness over MCP, the synthesis no longer needs to live inside the server. I moved the daily brief out to a client agent that drives the same MCP tools, leaving the server as pure data and deterministic compute. Here is the full build, plus the siege finding that a 'read-only' SQL guard wasn't read-only.

entries.title: Make MCP the front door: one server, a read-only fence, and a localhost that still needs locks
entries.summary: I put my self-hosted fitness data behind the Model Context Protocol so I can query and update it straight from an AI client. Here is the full build: defining tools and resources, serving one server over two transports, fencing the brief loop to read-only at the structure level, and the security a localhost MCP server actually needs.

entries.title: Guarantees the model can't break: shipping AI training plans design-first
entries.summary: I shipped goal-driven training plans where the AI can draft but never decide, a single active plan is enforced by the database itself, and adherence is graded off the last day data actually synced. The throughline: put the things you can't afford to get wrong into structure, not into prompts or discipline.

entries.title: A prompt is code you can't compile, so score it against the schema it feeds
entries.summary: I turned a personal Garmin-coaching script into real software: a scored prompt, a hermetic test core, and version-driven releases. The reusable idea is treating the agent's prompt as a testable artifact and cross-validating it against the Pydantic schema its output is checked against, so drift is caught in CI instead of crashing a user's briefing in production. Here is the full build.
